chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Computer Systems Design. (1-6). Credit 3. Engineering design; working as a design-team member, conceptual design methodology, design evaluations, total project planning and management techniques, design optimization, systems manufacturing costs considerations; emphasis placed upon students' activities as design professionals. Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331, and CSCE 462 or ECEN 449, and ECEN 325; senior classification.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331, and CSCE 462 or ECEN 449, and ECEN 325; senior classification.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
It is expected that successful participation in the course will allow the student to demonstrate:
• an ability to apply knowledge of mathematics, science, and engineering (3.a)
• an ability to design and conduct experiments, as well as to analyze and interpret data (3.b)
• an ability to design a system, component, or process to meet desired needs (3.c)
• an ability to function on multi-disciplinary teams (3.d)
• an ability to identify, formulate, and solve engineering problems (3.e)
• an understanding of professional and ethical responsibility (3.f)
• an ability to communicate effectively (3.g)
• an ability to use the techniques, skills, and modern engineering tools necessary for engineering practice (3.k)
Moreover, course-specific outcomes are
1. understand and utilize the engineering design method to generate potential solutions to a specific design challenge;
2. understand the basic of project management including: statements of work, work break-down structures, critical path identification, distribution of tasks, project tracking and status reporting; be able to generate status reports;
3. understand professional communication methods and techniques including formal reports and presentations;
4. clearly communicate ideas, status and test results through written reports;
5. clearly communicate through oral presentations.
6. to rigorously define design requirements for potential solutions.
7. understand and incorporate engineering standards and multiple realistic constraints that include most of the following considerations: economic, environmental, sustainability, manufacturability, ethical, health and safety, social and political.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content:
UCRT - University Required Oral Communication
C (communication-intensive course)
As a communication-intensive course, you will receive instruction on writing and public speaking and complete assignments so you can practice communication related to the major. You must pass the written and oral components with a C or better to earn passing grade in the course.
Failure to pass the communication components with a C or better results in an F for the course.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
| Rounded % total | Letter Grade |
|---|---|
| 100 - 90 | A |
| <90 and ≥ 80 | B |
| <80 and ≥ 70 | C |
| <70 and ≥ 60 | D |
| <60 | F |
This table captures deliverables, components of those

|---|---|---|
| 1 Status Reports | Progress: What was completed this week, Goals: What is planned for next week, Issues: What are the key issues/challenges that the project faces, Schedule: Are we on schedule for completion of intermediate milestones/final milestone | Written C Communi Problem I Decompo managem |
| 2 Minimum Viable Product Definition | Define scope of first product output, define completion date (usually no further out than 7 weeks into semester), define how much testing you will do, define acquisitio how different personas (atleast 2, preferably 3) will use the product | Written C Identificat Scoping |

| 5 Change Management | Keep track of what changes are required for the project based on learning, what changes were approved by stakeholders, and how that changes the shape of the final product | Scoping, Identificat Problem : Testing |

| 8 Final Product Completion | Product alignment to definition, demonstrate functionality with respect to defined personas, discuss testing methods and coverage, discuss gaps | Core Soft Testing |

| 12 Personal Contribution and Peer feedback | Your ability to function within a team collaboratively, your willingness to extend when needed, your ability to lead when required, your ability to follow when required, your ability to communicate frankly but constructively, your personal contribution to the project | Teamworl |

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
• No late submission of reports, milestones or code from published dates.
• If there is a good reason, we need to talk during class hours. You need to document and send email to myself and the TA. I will respond with an explicit approval.
• If you or your team submits a milestone late without approval you will lose the following % points from your awarded score:
  • Late by no more than 3 days, 80% of max awarded score
  • Late by 3+ days but no more than 1 week, 60% of max awarded score
  • Late by 1 week but no more than 2 weeks, 40% of max awarded score
  • Late by 2 weeks but no more than 3 weeks, 20% of max awarded score
  • Late by greater than 3 weeks, 0%
• Final milestone and poster milestone cannot be late. Zero for delayed final milestone and poster milestone submission.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
•All detected plagiarism, cheating, and complicity will be reported to the Aggie Honor System Office.
•The sanction is a zero on the assignment and a letter grade reduction for a single infraction.
•The sanction can be a letter grade F or F* for a pattern of infractions.
•Academic dishonesty will not be tolerated. For assignments, such as status reports or poster creation, the group is expected to write their material. Do not use Al tools! Not even for polishing your report or correcting grammar. Use spellcheckers and grammar checkers in document editors.
•Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any portion of any assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write assignments and using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT, Gemini.
•Every student should understand that complicity – helping or attempting to help another student commit an act of academic dishonesty – also constitutes academic dishonesty and carries the same punishment as cheating and plagiarism.
•Example if you have direct knowledge that another person on the class is using Al tools for unapproved work, and you do not report it immediately, you will be subject to the same sanction as the individual committing plagiarism or unauthorized access of Al tools.
•Use of Chatgpt for coding: If you plan to use chatgpt to write any part of your code, you need to discuss it with me, make clear and cogent arguments why this is the best use of the team's time, and we need to come to an agreement, this has to be clearly documented.
•Use of Chatgpt for search and exploration is permitted. However, no code should be generated. No reports should be generated. No cut-and-paste should be employed. Any data should be retrieved from the original source.
•You should setup explicit guardrails (boundaries) that ensure there is “no creep” i.e. gradual increase the footprint of chatgpt code as the project progresses, beyond predefined boundaries.
•You need to email to me with all team members and get an approval from me (the instructor). This cannot be done post “the act” of Al use. That will be treated as an infraction.
•Example scenario: I will allow use of Al for a very limited number of use cases where the code being generated is not fundamental or core to the project success, but is ancillary or plays a supporting role. An example maybe generating the use of a UI, where the core of the project is not UI. Again, this has to be discussed and preapproved!
•When in doubt discuss with your TA or instructor.
•Always ensure proper, timely approvals of exceptions.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content: Each student gets 3 free absences from attendance If you miss more than 3 class room sessions and you do not have a medical excuse or critical personal excuse, documented and approved, 1% of total score will be deducted per missed absence above this limit.

chunks.title: Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content: Since this is a team and project based course there is no makeup policy. For special situations talk to the Instructor and get approved makeup assignments.
